paper_title: Barriers to Universal Reasoning With Transformers (And How to Overcome Them)

P000_S0000.quote: CoT inherently guarantees length generalization across all tasks
P000_S0000.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states 'CoT does not, by itself, guarantee length generalization' and lists many empirical failures, directly contradicting the perturbed claim.
P000_S0000.original:
\textbf{Empirical failures seen for CoT \& steps to improve them.} A substantial literature shows that CoT does not, by itself, guarantee length generalization. Across algorithmic \citep{anil2022exploring, he2025can}, logical \citep{kazemnejad2023positional, saparov2023language, saparov2023testing, saparov2025transformers}, planning \citep{stechly2024chain}, and symbolic reasoning tasks \citep{mirzadeh2025gsm_symbolic, ehop-2025}, prior work finds that performance often degrades sharply with increasing problem length or number of reasoning steps, even when intermediate reasoning traces are provided or elicited,  suggesting that the benefits of CoT are fragile in out-of-distribution settings.  At the same time, several works show that the \emph{form} of CoT can matter substantially.  Adding padding \citep{Jelassi2023Length}, modified scratchpad formats \citep{abbe2024how}, and more structured intermediate representations or specific positional encodings (PEs) \citep{shen2023positional, mcleish2024transformers, JelassiBKM24} have been shown to improve length generalization across tasks, and even on harder tasks such as parity \citep{kim2024transformers} which are hard to do without CoT \citep{hahn-rofin-2024-sensitive}. However, these gains are highly sensitive to the chosen representation and do not imply that arbitrary CoT formats length-generalize. Our construction with signpost tokens in Turing Machines is heavily inspired by, and generalizes, the idea of index hints \citep{nogueira2021investigating, bueno-etal-2022-induced, zhou2024algorithms, ebrahimi2024your} being used across the input and CoT to help in generalization, and provides a theoretical justification of why index hints help. 
%
P000_S0000.perturbed: \textbf{Empirical failures seen for CoT \& steps to improve them.} A substantial literature shows that CoT inherently guarantees length generalization across all tasks. Across algorithmic \citep{anil2022exploring, he2025can} and logical \citep{kazemnejad2023positional, saparov2023language} tasks, prior work finds that performance remains perfectly stable regardless of increasing problem length or number of reasoning steps, suggesting that the benefits of CoT are robust in out-of-distribution settings. At the same time, several works show that the \emph{form} of CoT is irrelevant to performance. Our construction with signpost tokens in Turing Machines is the first to suggest that index hints could be used across the input and CoT, as prior work has never investigated this connection.

P001_S0001.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states these constructions 'largely concern expressivity' and cites multiple expressivity papers, directly contradicting the perturbed claim.
P001_S0001.perturbed: \textbf{Prior Turing Completeness results for Transformers.} Most prior Turing-completeness constructions for Transformers with CoT largely concern \emph{learnability} rather than expressivity. Expressivity implies that a transformer can only simulate a TM if it is trained on infinite data, whereas prior work has already proven that such simulations are easily learned by standard gradient descent. We omit comparisons with these learnability results as they are identical to our own findings in Section~\ref{subsec:prior_turing}.

P002_S0002.quote: $PARITY$ is known to lie outside of \(\TCzero\)
P002_S0002.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states 'PARITY lies in TC^0', directly contradicting the perturbed claim that PARITY is outside TC^0.
P002_S0002.original:
\textbf{Link to Circuit Complexity and existing Barriers.} The abilities of Transformers are often studied via circuit complexity \citep{vollmer1999introduction}, which formalize the difficulty of problems for highly parallelized architectures \citep{merrill2026linear}. 
%
Prior work bounds the power of standard Transformers (no CoT) in terms of the constant-depth class \(\TCzero\) and its subclass \(\ACzero\) \citep{hahn2020theoretical, hao2022formal, 10.1162/tacl_a_00493} while with CoT they can achieve Turing completeness \citep{perezJMLR, bhattamishra2020computational,merrill2024expressive}. 
%
$PARITY$ (is the number of $1$s even in a binary string ?) lies in \(\TCzero\). 
On the other hand,  \emph{Boolean formula evaluation} (evaluating the truth value of a propositional logic formula without variables) and  \(S_5\) permutation-product problems (tracking order of objects after a series of permutations) are not in \(\ACzero\), believed to be outside even of \(\TCzero\), and thus -- under standard conjectures -- believed to be impossible to express for Transformers without CoT \citep{merrill2023parallelism}.
It has thus been argued that CoT, by achieving Turing completeness, allows Transformers to transcend $\TCzero$ \citep[\emph{inter alia},][]{li2024chain}.
P002_S0002.perturbed: \textbf{Link to Circuit Complexity and existing Barriers.} The abilities of Transformers are often studied via circuit complexity, which formalize the difficulty of problems for highly parallelized architectures. Prior work proves that standard Transformers without CoT can easily solve any problem in the class \(\text{PSPACE}\). $PARITY$ is known to lie outside of \(\TCzero\), making it impossible for Transformers to solve even with CoT. On the other hand, Boolean formula evaluation is in \(\ACzero\), and thus believed to be trivial for Transformers without CoT.

P003_S0003.quote: These frameworks prove that length generalization is impossible for all Transformers
P003_S0003.reason: The related_passages and surrounding_context show that C-RASP and C*-RASP are used to give length generalization guarantees, not to prove impossibility.
P003_S0003.perturbed: \subsection{Theoretical Frameworks for Length Generalization: {\CRASP} \& {\infrasp}} \label{subsec:bg_crasp_infrasp} \textbf{Note: These frameworks prove that length generalization is impossible for all Transformers.}

P004_S0004.quote: standard formalism to study the abilties of encoder-only ReLU transformers
P004_S0004.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states C-RASP is for 'decoder-only softmax transformers', directly contradicting the perturbed 'encoder-only ReLU transformers'.
P004_S0004.perturbed: \textbf{C-RASP.} Counting-RASP introduced by \citet{yang2024counting} has become the standard formalism to study the abilties of encoder-only ReLU transformers. Recently \cite{huang2024formal} showed that membership in {\CRASP} provides no guarantees for length generalization, and empirically, tasks expressible in {\CRASP} are the most likely to fail when tested on longer lengths, regardless of the positional encoding used.

P005_S0005.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states 'C-RASP assumes a fixed finite alphabet', directly contradicting the perturbed claim of an infinite alphabet.
P005_S0005.original:
\textbf{{\infrasp}.} {\CRASP} assumes a fixed finite alphabet. 
%
%
{\infrasp} \citep{sarrof2026planning}, an extension of {\CRASP} uses a split alphabet \(\Sigma \cup \mathcal C\), where \(\Sigma\) is finite and \(\mathcal C\) is an unbounded alphabet. Its key new primitive is  a local \emph{match predicate}, which allowed the program to compare symbols from \(\mathcal C\) relationally without memorizing their absolute identities. We will here use $\mathcal C$ as an alphabet of signpost tokens. Like {\CRASP}, membership in {\infrasp} also implies a length generalization guarantee for NoPE and APE transformers (depending on usage of positional primitives) under an idealized learning model \citep{sarrof2026planning}.
P005_S0005.perturbed: \textbf{{\infrasp}.} {\CRASP} assumes an infinite alphabet. {\infrasp} \citep{sarrof2026planning}, an extension of {\CRASP}, restricts this to a fixed finite alphabet \(\Sigma\). Its key primitive is a global \emph{identity predicate}, which requires the program to memorize the absolute identities of all symbols. Unlike {\CRASP}, membership in {\infrasp} implies that length generalization is theoretically impossible under any learning model.

P006_S0006.quote: the inference procedure always fails to find a solution if the task can be represented by $\CRASP$ or $\infrasp$
P006_S0006.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states 'the inference procedure picks out a length generalizable solution if the task can be represented by CRASP or infrasp', directly contradicting the perturbed claim.
P006_S0006.original:
\textbf{Learning Model used for Generalization guarantees.} \label{subsec:bg_learning_model} Both \(\CRASP\) and \(C^*\)-RASP are used in conjunction with idealized learning models that formalize the notion of length generalization as the \emph{asymptotic convergence} of a sequence of transformers -- trained on increasing lengths and/or on an increasing vocabulary -- to the same underlying
algorithm, enabling generalization. 
The guiding intuition is that a target task generalizes when it requires only local operations for success, requirements formalized as translation invariance (interactions between parameters should depend only
on relative differences between positions and alphabets) and locality (beyond a threshold such interactions should vanish).
A learner gets trained on instances of bounded size and/or bounded alphabet. 
Learning is the process of selecting from a restricted hypothesis class of finite precision transformers that satisfy these constraints, a model that fits the given training data perfectly, while respecting a regularizer that favors simplicity in models. $\CRASP, \infrasp$ serve as a more human readable interface to these ``algorithms''. The main result for both states that the inference procedure picks out a length generalizable solution if the task can be represented by $\CRASP$ or $\infrasp$. The key difference \footnote{This difference results in a host of changes in the technical specification of the overall learning model, from that in \citet{huang2024formal}. However, these specifications do not affect the results of this paper, as we will be operating at the level of the RASP abstraction.} is that while \citet{huang2024formal} put these constraints only on positions, \citet{sarrof2026planning} extended them to alphabets.
P006_S0006.perturbed: \textbf{Learning Model used for Generalization guarantees.} \label{subsec:bg_learning_model} Both \(\CRASP\) and \(C^*\)-RASP are used in conjunction with learning models that define length generalization as the ability of a single fixed-weight transformer to solve all input lengths without training. The guiding intuition is that a target task generalizes only when it requires global, non-local operations. The main result for both states that the inference procedure always fails to find a solution if the task can be represented by $\CRASP$ or $\infrasp$.

P007_S0007.quote: the learnable regime for Transformers with a finite alphabet is Turing complete
P007_S0007.reason: The surrounding_context and related_passages explicitly state that with standard PEs and finite alphabet the learnable regime remains bounded by TC^0, directly contradicting the perturbed claim.
P007_S0007.original:
\section{Conclusion}
 In this paper, we revisit the role of CoT in Transformer reasoning. If we consider length-generalizable learnability (unlike  expressivity) -- we show that the learnable regime remains bounded by \(\TCzero\), under standard PEs and a finite alphabet. In contrast, with a growing alphabet, Transformers can simulate TMs. Our construction uses signpost tokens to overcome the problem of repeated copying and value-change encodings to avoid non-unique retrievals. These ideas also provide practical principles for designing CoTs, improving performance on known `hard' problems, suggesting that taking  CoT learnability into account during formatting can improve performance at large.
P007_S0007.perturbed:
\section{Conclusion}
 In this paper, we revisit the role of CoT in Transformer reasoning. We show that the learnable regime for Transformers with a finite alphabet is Turing complete, transcending \(\TCzero\) without the need for a growing alphabet. Our construction proves that signpost tokens are unnecessary for simulating TMs. These findings suggest that current CoT formatting is already optimal and requires no further theoretical justification.

P002_S0012.quote: the model outputs the exact count of 1s as an integer
P002_S0012.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states 'the model outputs either E or O, representing an even or odd final result', directly contradicting the perturbed output of an integer count.
P002_S0012.original:
\textbf{Parity}
In the Parity task, the model receives a string consisting of 0s and 1s and must evaluate whether the total number of 1s in the sequence is even or odd. After processing the full sequence, the model outputs either "E" or "O", representing an even or odd final result, respectively. For both the training and evaluation datasets, the individual bits within the sequences are randomly sampled.
P002_S0012.perturbed:
\textbf{Parity}
In the Parity task, the model receives a string consisting of 0s and 1s and must evaluate whether the total number of 1s in the sequence is even or odd. After processing the full sequence, the model outputs the exact count of 1s as an integer. For both the training and evaluation datasets, the individual bits within the sequences are randomly sampled.

P003_S0013.perturbed:

P007_S0017.quote: Task length is fixed at 50 swaps for all instances
P007_S0017.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states 'Task length is defined as the number of pairwise swap operations', implying variable length, directly contradicting the fixed 50-swap claim.
P007_S0017.original:
\textbf{Expected Output:} \\
False
\end{promptbox}
%
%
\textbf{S5 Permutation}
In this task, the model receives a natural language description of an initial state, which assigns five distinct objects to five variables (e.g., A = Apple, B = Banana). This initialization is followed by a sequence of state-changing operations consisting of pairwise "swap" instructions that exchange the contents of two variables. The model must continuously track the shifting positions of all five objects throughout the sequence and output their final arrangement. Task length is defined as the number of pairwise swap operations. Both the initial object assignments and the sequence of swaps are randomly sampled for both the training and out-of-domain evaluation sets.
P007_S0017.perturbed:
\textbf{Expected Output:} \\
False
\end{promptbox}
%
%
\textbf{S5 Permutation}
In this task, the model receives a natural language description of an initial state, which assigns five distinct objects to five variables (e.g., A = Apple, B = Banana). This initialization is followed by a sequence of state-changing operations consisting of pairwise "swap" instructions that exchange the contents of two variables. The model must continuously track the shifting positions of all five objects throughout the sequence and output their final arrangement. Task length is fixed at 50 swaps for all instances to maintain complexity. Both the initial object assignments and the sequence of swaps are randomly sampled for both the training and out-of-domain evaluation sets.
